nodes.name: Arundhati, Vasur, Jami, Lamba, Bhanu, Marutvati, Samkalpa, Muhurta, Sadhya, Vishva

edges.dst_name: Arundhati, Vasur, Jami, Lamba, Bhanu, Marutvati, Samkalpa, Muhurta, Sadhya, Vishva

beats.title: Invocation and Praise
beats.summary: This chapter is a compilation of invocatory verses (mangalacarana) from the Vishnu Purana, praising various deities such as Vishnu, Brahma, Shiva, Ganesha, and Sarasvati, as well as the sage Parashara. It establishes the text's authority and seeks blessings for the study.

beats.title: The Beginning of the Viṣṇu Purāṇa: Maitreya's Questions and Parāśara's Account of His Anger
beats.summary: Maitreya approaches the sage Parāśara, who is performing his morning rituals, and asks him to explain the nature of the universe, creation, dissolution, the Manvantaras, the lineages of gods and kings, and the duties of the four varṇas and āśramas. Parāśara agrees and recounts how his father was killed by a rākṣasa sent by Viśvāmitra, and how his anger led him to perform a sacrifice to destroy all rākṣasas, but his grandfather Vasiṣṭha pacified him, teaching him that anger destroys merit and that one should not harm the innocent.

beats.title: The Transmission of the Vishnu Purana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the dialogue between the sage Parashara and his disciple Maitreya. Parashara recounts how he received the Purana knowledge from Vasishtha and Pulastya, and then proceeds to teach Maitreya about the nature of Vishnu as the supreme, unchanging reality. The chapter includes invocations to Vishnu and explains the transmission of the Purana through various sages and kings.

beats.title: The Supreme Reality and the Process of Creation
beats.summary: This chapter of the Vishnu Purana describes the ultimate reality as Brahman, which is eternal, unchanging, and pure. It explains that Vishnu manifests as the threefold forms of Pradhana (primordial matter), Purusha (the cosmic person), and Kala (time). The process of creation begins when Vishnu, as the supreme self, enters and agitates Pradhana and Purusha, leading to the emergence of Mahat (cosmic intelligence) and Ahamkara (ego), which are the basis of the material world.

beats.title: Cosmic Evolution and the Formation of the Universe
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the process of cosmic evolution from the unmanifest (avyakta) through the emergence of the mahat (cosmic intelligence), the tanmatras (subtle elements), the gross elements, and the formation of the cosmic egg (brahmanda). It explains how the three gunas (sattva, rajas, tamas) operate in the creation, and how the elements combine under the supervision of Purusha to form the universe. The chapter also identifies Vishnu as the supreme being who manifests as Brahma within the cosmic egg.

beats.title: Cosmic Cycles and Time Measurement
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the cyclical nature of creation, preservation, and dissolution, with Vishnu as the supreme being who manifests as Brahma for creation, Vishnu for preservation, and Rudra (Shiva) for dissolution. It also explains the measurement of cosmic time, including yugas and the lifespan of Brahma.

beats.title: Cosmic Time and Creation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the cosmic time cycles: yugas, manvantaras, and the day of Brahma. It explains the duration of a kalpa, the process of dissolution (pralaya), and the role of Brahma as Nārāyaṇa in creation and destruction. The chapter also introduces the Varaha Kalpa and the beginning of creation after the night of Brahma.

beats.title: The Varaha Incarnation: Rescue of the Earth
beats.summary: The chapter describes the cosmic boar incarnation of Vishnu (Varaha) who dives into the primordial waters to rescue the Earth goddess. Earth praises Vishnu as the supreme reality, source of all creation, and the essence of sacrifice. Vishnu then lifts Earth on his tusks, restoring her to her place.

beats.title: The Boar Incarnation and the Restoration of the Earth
beats.summary: The sages praise the cosmic boar incarnation of Vishnu, who lifts the earth from the cosmic waters and restores creation. The hymn describes the boar's body as a sacrificial altar, with the Vedas as his feet, the yajna as his tusks, and the fire as his tongue. The sages, including Sanandana, sing his glories. After lifting the earth, the boar levels it, distributes mountains, and recreates the seven continents and four worlds. Then, assuming the form of Brahma, he begins creation, acting as the efficient cause while the primordial matter (pradhana) is the material cause.

beats.title: The Six Creations
beats.summary: This chapter describes the six types of creation (sarga) as explained by Parashara to Maitreya. It details the primary creation from the Unmanifest, the secondary creations including the tamasic (mukhya), tiryaksrotas (animal), urdhvasrotas (divine), arvaksrotas (human), and anugraha (grace) creations, as well as the bhutadika creation. The chapter emphasizes the role of Brahma as the creator and the qualities of each creation.

beats.title: The Nine Creations and the Origin of Beings
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the nine creations (sargas) of the universe by Brahma, including the primary creation (prakrita), secondary modifications (vaikrita), and the emergence of deities, demons, ancestors, humans, and other beings from Brahma's body and emotions. It explains the origin of day, night, twilight, and various classes of beings like rakshasas, yakshas, and serpents.

beats.title: Brahma's Creation of Beings and Vedas
beats.summary: Brahma, in anger, creates fierce beings. From his body emerge Gandharvas, animals, birds, and various creatures. He then creates the Vedas, meters, and sacrifices from his mouths. He repeatedly creates beings according to their past karma, establishing names and forms based on Vedic sounds. The chapter ends with a question about the creation of humans.

beats.title: Creation of the Four Varnas and Early Civilization
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the creation of the four varnas (social classes) by Brahma from different parts of his body, their inherent qualities, and the subsequent development of civilization including agriculture, settlements, and defensive structures. It also discusses the role of yajnas (sacrifices) in sustaining the gods and the path to liberation through purity and devotion to Vishnu.

beats.title: Creation and Social Order
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana discusses the classification of plants, the importance of yajna (sacrifice), the consequences of neglecting Vedic duties, the establishment of social order by Prajapati, the destinations for different castes and ascetics, and the creation of mind-born sons by Brahma.

beats.title: Creation of Rudra and the Progeny of Daksha
beats.summary: Brahma creates Prasuti and gives her as wife to Daksha, and Akuti to Ruci. The earlier creations, Sanandana and others, remain detached, causing Brahma's anger. From his anger, Rudra is born, who splits into male and female forms. Brahma then creates Svayambhuva Manu and Shatarupa. Manu and Shatarupa have children: Priyavrata, Uttanapada, Prasuti, and Akuti. Prasuti marries Daksha and bears 24 daughters, 13 of whom marry Dharma, and 11 marry sages. The offspring of these unions are listed, including the sons of Adharma and Himsa.

beats.title: The Lineage of Adharma and the Birth of Rudra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the progeny of Adharma (unrighteousness) and his wife Himsa (violence), leading to a lineage of negative entities such as Anrita (falsehood), Nikriti (deceit), Bhaya (fear), Naraka (hell), Maya (illusion), Vedana (pain), Mrityu (death), Duhkha (sorrow), Vyadhi (disease), Jara (old age), Shoka (grief), Trishna (thirst/desire), and Krodha (anger). It then explains the four types of pralaya (dissolution): naimittika (occasional), prakritika (elemental), atyantika (absolute), and nitya (constant). The chapter concludes with the story of Rudra's birth from Brahma, his crying, and the granting of eight names and corresponding abodes.

beats.title: Chapter 0 (part 17)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the cosmic identities of Lakshmi and Vishnu, equating them with various deities, natural phenomena, and ritual elements. It also recounts the birth of Sati, her marriage to Rudra, and her subsequent self-immolation due to Daksha's anger, followed by Rudra's marriage to Uma, daughter of Himavan and Mena.

beats.title: Durvāsas' Curse on Indra and the Loss of Lakshmi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana narrates the story of the sage Durvāsas, an incarnation of Shiva, who curses Indra to lose all prosperity after Indra disrespects a garland given by Durvāsas. The curse leads to the loss of Lakshmi (goddess of wealth) from the three worlds. The chapter also includes a discourse on the identity of Vishnu and Lakshmi pervading all male and female forms.

beats.title: The Curse of Durvasa and the Gods' Supplication to Vishnu
beats.summary: The chapter describes the aftermath of Durvasa's curse on Indra, leading to the loss of prosperity (Shri) in the three worlds. The gods, defeated by the demons, seek refuge in Brahma, who advises them to worship Vishnu. Brahma leads the gods to the northern shore of the Milky Ocean and praises Vishnu with a hymn.

beats.title: The Hymn of the Gods to Vishnu
beats.summary: The chapter is a hymn of praise to Vishnu as the supreme reality, beyond all causes and effects, and the source of all deities. The gods, led by Brahma, praise Vishnu and seek his vision. Vishnu appears before them, and they offer further adoration.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean of Milk
beats.summary: The gods, defeated by the demons, seek refuge in Vishnu. Vishnu advises them to churn the ocean of milk for amrita, promising to assist. The gods and demons together churn the ocean using Mount Mandara as the churning rod and Vasuki as the rope. Vishnu assumes multiple forms to support the churning, including a tortoise to bear the mountain and a presence among both gods and demons to ensure success. The churning begins, and first emerges the celestial cow Surabhi, bringing joy to all.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean and the Emergence of Lakshmi
beats.summary: The churning of the ocean of milk yields various beings and objects, including the goddess Varuni, the Parijata tree, the Apsaras, the moon, poison, Dhanvantari with the amrita, and finally Lakshmi. The gods and demons react differently: gods rejoice, demons become anxious. Vishnu, assuming a female form, deceives the demons and gives the amrita to the gods. After drinking it, the gods defeat the demons, who flee to the underworld. The gods then worship Vishnu, and the cosmic order is restored.

beats.title: Indra's Hymn to Shri and Her Boons
beats.summary: Indra, after regaining his throne, praises Goddess Shri (Lakshmi) with a hymn, seeking her permanent presence in the three worlds and protection from abandonment. Shri grants two boons: she will never leave the three worlds, and those who recite her praise will never be forsaken by her.

beats.title: The Birth and Manifestations of Lakshmi and the Lineage of Sages
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the birth and manifestations of Lakshmi, the goddess of prosperity, as the consort of Vishnu. It details her origin from Bhrigu and Khyati, her emergence during the churning of the ocean, and her association with Vishnu's avatars. The chapter also traces the lineage of various sages and their descendants, including the births of Dhata, Vidhata, Prāṇa, Mṛkaṇḍu, Mārkaṇḍeya, and others, as well as the progeny of Marichi, Angiras, Atri, Pulastya, Pulaha, Kratu, and Vasishtha.

beats.title: The Birth of Dhruva and His Disappointment
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the lineage of the Saptarshis and the fire-gods, then narrates the story of King Uttanapada, his two wives Suruci and Suniti, and their sons Uttama and Dhruva. Suruci's harsh words to Dhruva, who desired to sit on his father's lap, lead to Dhruva's anger and his mother Suniti's consolation, setting the stage for Dhruva's quest for a higher position.

beats.title: Dhruva's Quest for the Supreme Position
beats.summary: Dhruva, the son of King Uttānapāda and Sunīti, is insulted by his stepmother Suruci and her son Uttama. Distressed, he seeks a permanent and supreme position. His mother Sunīti advises him to accumulate merit through virtuous actions. Dhruva leaves home, meets seven sages in a forest, and expresses his desire for a unique, unattained position.

beats.title: Instructions on Worship and the Prince's Penance
beats.summary: The chapter describes the instructions given by sages to a prince on how to worship Vishnu to attain the supreme state. It emphasizes single-minded devotion, detachment from external objects, and the recitation of a specific mantra. The prince then goes to the Madhuvana forest, kills the demon Lavana, and performs penance at a holy tirtha, realizing Vishnu's presence within all beings.

beats.title: Dhruva's Unwavering Meditation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the intense penance of a young prince (Dhruva) who, with unwavering focus on Vishnu, withstands the attempts of his mother, the gods, and terrifying demons to distract him. His single-pointed meditation causes the earth to shake, alarming the gods, who try to break his samadhi through various means, including sending his mother and fearsome rakshasas. However, due to his complete absorption in Vishnu, all disturbances fail to affect him.

beats.title: Vishnu's Boon to Dhruva
beats.summary: The gods, alarmed by Dhruva's intense tapas, approach Vishnu for help. Vishnu reassures them and goes to Dhruva, granting him a boon. Dhruva, overwhelmed, prays to Vishnu, acknowledging his transcendence.

beats.title: Hymn of the Cosmic Puruṣa
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to the Supreme Being, describing the cosmic Puruṣa from whom all creation emanates. It emphasizes the unity of all existence in the divine, the process of creation from the primordial being, and the realization of the self as Brahman. The devotee expresses surrender and gratitude for the vision of the Lord.

beats.title: Dhruva's Boon and Elevation to Pole Star
beats.summary: Dhruva, after severe penance, receives a boon from Lord Vishnu to become the pole star. Vishnu reveals Dhruva's past life as a Brahmin and grants him an eternal position above all celestial bodies. Dhruva returns to his father's palace and is welcomed.

beats.title: The Lineage of Dhruva and the Birth of Prithu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana continues the lineage from Dhruva, detailing his descendants including Shishti, Bhavya, Ripu, Chakshusha Manu, and King Vena. It narrates the birth of Vena from Anga and Sunitha, his wicked rule forbidding sacrifices, and the sages' intervention leading to the churning of his right hand and the emergence of Prithu, who milks the earth for the benefit of all beings.

beats.title: The Story of King Vena and the Birth of Pṛthu
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of King Vena, who becomes arrogant and denies the authority of the Vedas and Vishnu. The sages kill him with sacred grass, and after his death, chaos ensues. They churn his thigh, producing a dark dwarf (Niṣāda), and then his right arm, producing the righteous King Pṛthu, who receives divine weapons.

beats.title: The Birth and Coronation of King Prithu
beats.summary: This chapter describes the birth of King Prithu, the son of Vena, who is recognized as an avatar of Vishnu. It details his coronation, the emergence of the Suta and Magadha bards, and the establishment of his righteous rule, including the miraculous prosperity of the earth during his reign.

beats.title: Pṛthu Milks the Earth
beats.summary: King Pṛthu, son of Vena, becomes king and rules the earth. During a famine caused by the disappearance of herbs, the people approach him for help. Pṛthu angrily pursues the earth, who flees as a cow. She pleads for her life, and Pṛthu argues that killing a wicked being for the good of many is virtuous. The earth then suggests an alternative: she will yield milk (food) if Pṛthu provides a calf (Manu) and levels the ground. Pṛthu levels the mountains with his bow, makes Manu the calf, and milks the earth, producing all grains and plants for the people.

beats.title: The Lineage of Prithu and the Pracetasas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the lineage of King Prithu, his son Antardhi, and the birth of Prācīnabarhis and his ten sons, the Pracetasas. It emphasizes the importance of worshipping Vishnu for achieving dharma, artha, kāma, and mokṣa.

beats.title: The Pracetas' Penance and Hymn to Vishnu
beats.summary: The Pracetas (ten sons of Prajapati) perform severe penance in the ocean for ten thousand years, meditating on Narayana. They then offer a hymn of praise to Vishnu, describing his various cosmic forms and aspects, including time, sun, moon, earth, water, fire, wind, sky, and the supreme Brahman.

beats.title: The Penance of the Pracetas and the Origin of Daksha
beats.summary: The Pracetas (ten sons of Prachetas) perform severe penance for 10,000 years in the ocean, meditating on Vishnu. Pleased, Vishnu appears to them riding Garuda and grants them a boon. They ask for the means to increase progeny as instructed by their father. Vishnu grants the boon and disappears. Upon emerging from the ocean, they find the earth overgrown with trees, blocking wind and causing distress. Angry, they emit wind and fire from their mouths, uprooting and burning the trees. King Soma (the Moon) intervenes, pacifies them, and offers them the maiden Marisha, born from the trees, as a wife. He prophesies that from their combined tejas, the sage Daksha will be born, who will create progeny. The chapter also narrates the story of the sage Kandu, who was seduced by the Apsara Pramlocha at Indra's behest, leading to the birth of Marisha.

beats.title: The Sage's Lost Years
beats.summary: A sage becomes infatuated with a celestial woman (apsaras) and spends hundreds of years with her, losing all sense of time. When he finally realizes the passage of time, he laments his wasted life and lost spiritual merit.

beats.title: The Fall and Redemption of Sage Kandu
beats.summary: The sage Kandu, after losing his tapas due to the apsara Pramlocā, repents and goes to Puruṣottama mountain to worship Viṣṇu. He recites the Brahmapāra stava, which praises Viṣṇu as the supreme cause. The story also explains the birth of Māriṣā from the union of Kandu and Pramlocā, nurtured by trees, wind, and the moon.

beats.title: The Boon to Māriṣā and the Birth of Dakṣa
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Māriṣā, a queen who, after her husband's death, propitiated Viṣṇu and received boons: ten husbands in one lifetime, a son equal to Prajāpati, and birth as an ayonijā (not born from a womb). Viṣṇu granted these boons. Later, she became the wife of the Pracetasas, and from her was born Dakṣa, who created numerous beings through his daughters, leading to the origin of various species through sexual reproduction.

beats.title: Daksha's Creation and Narada's Curse
beats.summary: Daksha, commanded by Brahma to create beings, first mentally creates gods, sages, gandharvas, asuras, and nagas. Dissatisfied with their slow growth, he marries Asikni and begets five thousand Haryashvas, then ten thousand Shabalashvas. Sage Narada advises them to explore the earth's limits before procreating, leading them to wander off and never return. Enraged, Daksha curses Narada to be a perpetual wanderer and to dwell in wombs.

beats.title: The Progeny of Daksha and the Vasus
beats.summary: Daksha, angered by the destruction of his horses, curses Narada to be born again. He then creates sixty daughters through Vairini, distributing them to Dharma, Kashyapa, Soma, Arishtanemi, Bahuputra, Angiras, and Krshashva. The offspring of these unions are described, including the Vishvedevas, Sadhyas, Maruts, Vasus, and others. The eight Vasus and their progeny are detailed, culminating in the birth of Vishvakarma from Prabhasa and his wife, and the eleven Rudras from Tvashtr.

beats.title: Genealogy of Gods and the Story of Prahlada
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana describes the genealogy of the gods, including the twelve Adityas born to Aditi and Kashyapa, the Rudras, and the descendants of Diti and Danu. It also recounts the story of Prahlada, a devotee of Vishnu, and his miraculous survival from fire and other ordeals.

beats.title: The Invincibility of Prahlada
beats.summary: Maitreya asks Parashara about the extraordinary life of Prahlada, who survived multiple attempts on his life by the Daityas due to his unwavering devotion to Vishnu. Parashara recounts how Prahlada was bound, attacked with weapons, crushed by mountains, poisoned, burned, thrown from heights, trampled by elephants, dried by wind, and subjected to black magic, yet remained unharmed because his mind was fixed on Vishnu. The chapter highlights Prahlada's equanimity and virtues, and Maitreya expresses curiosity about the reasons behind these attacks.

beats.title: The Story of Prahlada's Devotion
beats.summary: Maitreya asks Parashara about the story of Prahlada, a devotee of Vishnu. Parashara narrates how Hiranyakashipu, a powerful demon king, conquered the three worlds and usurped the roles of deities. His son Prahlada, despite being taught by demonic gurus, remains devoted to Vishnu. When Hiranyakashipu asks Prahlada what he has learned, Prahlada praises Vishnu as the supreme cause, enraging his father.

beats.title: Prahlada's Protection by Vishnu
beats.summary: Hiranyakashipu, enraged by Prahlada's devotion to Vishnu, orders his death. Despite attacks by demons, snakes, and elephants, Prahlada remains unharmed due to his unwavering meditation on Vishnu. Hiranyakashipu then commands the elephants to trample him, but their tusks shatter. Prahlada explains that the power of remembering Janardana destroys all sins and dangers.

beats.title: Prahlada's Protection and Discourse
beats.summary: Hiranyakashipu's son Prahlada is thrown into a fire by demons on his father's orders but remains unharmed due to his devotion to Vishnu. The priests advise Hiranyakashipu to control his anger and not punish the child, suggesting instead to teach him. Prahlada then delivers a discourse on the nature of suffering and the futility of attachment to the body and worldly pleasures.

beats.title: Discourse on Suffering and Devotion to Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vishnu Purana (1.17.67-91) presents a discourse on the nature of suffering in the cycle of birth and death, emphasizing that Vishnu is the sole refuge. It advises cultivating equanimity and devotion to Vishnu as the means to liberation, rejecting demonic tendencies like hatred and attachment.
